findings.title: Attachment defaults massively widen per-message download cost
findings.description: The new defaults raise attachment handling from 25 MiB / 50 MiB / 30 s to 500 MiB / 500 MiB / 120 s. In this bot, attachment download happens before the message is handed to the session worker, so one Discord message can now hold the turn open while consuming hundreds of megabytes of disk and network I/O. That is a practical DoS/regression risk for shared bots and makes race windows around mid-turn injection much longer than before.
findings.suggestion: Keep the old limits as defaults, or require explicit opt-in for larger values. If large files are required, add stronger backpressure such as per-guild/user caps, disk-budget checks, and a separate worker pool so attachment fetches do not block the main message flow.

findings.title: Unchecked MB-to-bytes conversion can overflow configured limits
findings.description: `max_file_size_mb * 1024 * 1024` and `max_aggregate_size_mb * 1024 * 1024` are unchecked `u64` multiplications. A large config value will panic in debug builds and wrap in release builds, producing a bogus byte limit. That can silently disable or distort the attachment guardrails you just added.
findings.suggestion: Validate attachment limits during `Config::load` and use `checked_mul` (or saturating arithmetic with an explicit error) when converting MB to bytes.

summary: The streaming change is mostly sound, but the new attachment configuration introduces a resource-exhaustion regression and unchecked limit arithmetic.
